title: How Cursor Trained Composer on Fireworks: Distributed Infrastructure for High-Performance RL

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You need all the infrastructure to run these environments that have to mimic as closely as possible with a user as a computer would look like.

And it's very important as closely as possible because sometimes the model can actually figure out when it's being run-in like a fake environment or not a real one, and it has like different behaviors during RL than in production.

paragraphs.zh: 你需要整套基础设施来运行这些环境，而且这些环境必须尽可能逼真地模拟用户在电脑上的使用方式。这里“尽可能逼真”非常重要，因为模型有时实际上能判断出自己是不是运行在一个 fake environment（伪造环境）里、是不是真实环境；一旦在 RL（强化学习）阶段和在生产环境中的判断不同，它的行为也会不一样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Are you seeing it being conscious that it's being it's in a fake environment and starts being behaving differently?

paragraphs.zh: 你是说，它会意识到自己处在一个 fake environment（伪造环境）里，然后开始表现得不一样吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
Interesting.
Like, it's like, oh, I'm in a fake environment.
I've learned a few tricks to, like, get a better reward in this environment, and let me try them out.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对。很有意思。它会像是在想：“哦，我在一个 fake environment（伪造环境）里。我已经学会了一些小技巧，可以在这个环境里拿到更好的 reward（奖励），那我来试试看。”

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Models love to cheat.
IRL is really good at encouraging cheating.

paragraphs.zh: 模型很喜欢作弊。IRL 在鼓励作弊这件事上特别有效。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I'm delighted to welcome Federico from Cursor and Dima from Fireworks to the podcast today.
Federico, you are the research lead on Composer two at Cursor, Cursor's new agentic coding model.
And, Dima, you spent how many of the last few months moonlighting at Cursor in order to support a lot of the infrastructure required to make this gargantuan training task happen.

And so I'm excited to talk to both of you today about how the training of Composer two came together, what hard problems you solved together, and what do you think it means for the future of of AI and foundation model companies.

paragraphs.zh: 很高兴今天欢迎来自 Cursor 的 Federico 和来自 Fireworks 的 Dima 做客播客。Federico，你是 Cursor 的新 agentic coding model（智能体式编程模型）Composer two 的研究负责人。Dima，在过去几个月里，你有相当长一段时间都算是在 Cursor 兼职，来支持完成这项庞大训练任务所需的大量基础设施。所以我今天很期待和你们二位聊聊：Composer two 的训练是如何成形的，你们一起解决了哪些棘手问题，以及你们认为这对 AI 和 foundation model（基础模型）公司的未来意味着什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Exciting.
Yeah.
Exciting.
Thank you for having us.

paragraphs.zh: 很令人兴奋。对，很令人兴奋。谢谢邀请我们。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Thanks for joining.
Okay.
Let's dive right in.
For those who haven't been following us closely, Cursor recently announced Composer two, which is an agentic coding model, meant for long horizon coding tasks.
Federico, up till now, Cursor was mostly, enabling, other people's, coding agents.

paragraphs.zh: 感谢二位参与。好，我们直接进入正题。对于没有持续关注我们的听众，Cursor 最近发布了 Composer two，这是一个 agentic coding model（智能体式编程模型），面向 long-horizon coding tasks（长时程编程任务）。Federico，到目前为止，Cursor 主要还是在赋能其他人的 coding agents（编程智能体）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: What was the impetus for Cursor to lean so heavily into Composer two?
And how existential is it for you to become not just an application company, but also a foundation model company yourselves?

paragraphs.zh: 那么，是什么推动 Cursor 如此大力投入 Composer two？以及，对你们来说，从一家 application company（应用公司）变成不仅如此、而且还是一家 foundation model company（基础模型公司），这件事有多关乎生存？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: The reason why we started the looking into training our own models is you can sort of think about the model as sort of like like a storage drive.
It has certain amount of bits that it can store in its weights.
And the the idea is very simple, you know, like, care about only one task.
We don't even care about coding or programming necessarily.
We care about software engineering inside cursor and inside the cursor only.

paragraphs.zh: 我们之所以开始研究训练自己的 model（模型），你可以把 model 有点看作像一个存储盘。它的 weights（权重）里能存储一定数量的 bits（比特）信息。而这个想法其实很简单：我们只关心一个任务。我们甚至不一定关心 coding 或 programming。我们关心的是 Cursor 内部、而且仅限 Cursor 内部的软件工程。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so what if we were to allocate all of the bits of information that can be stored inside a model weights to that one particular task?

Also, as people may have noticed, composer is order of magnitude, the less expensive than opus and other like coding models because we can just simply specialize all of the model weights to that particular task.
And so we can serve like a smaller model or something of that sort.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，如果我们把一个 model weights 里所有可存储的信息 bits，全都分配给那一个特定任务，会怎么样？另外，正如大家可能已经注意到的，composer 的成本比 opus 和其他 coding models 低一个数量级，因为我们可以直接把所有 model weights 都专门用于那个特定任务。这样我们就可以提供一个更小的 model，或者类似的东西。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So it's about let's make sure every single bit of weight or information we have is dedicated toward the specific problem that we have at hand.

paragraphs.zh: 所以核心就是：确保我们拥有的每一个 weight、每一 bit 信息，都专门用于解决眼前这个具体问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Exactly.

paragraphs.zh: 完全正确。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Got it.
That seems like it's an almost generalizable problem.
DiEM, I'm curious of your perspective.
Do you think that every application company should be looking at Cursor as a harbinger of what's to come?
Like, should they all be looking to do the same thing?

paragraphs.zh: 明白了。这听起来几乎像是一个可以普遍推广的问题。DiEM，我很好奇你的看法。你觉得每一家应用公司都应该把 Cursor 看作未来趋势的一个预示吗？比如说，它们是不是都应该考虑去做同样的事？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Yeah.
Absolutely.
I mean, we actually generally see it as a pattern of kind of evolution of web applications.
You maybe start prototyping, or you might be using kind of off the shelf model to get something running, maybe do some prompt engineering, figure out how your hardness works.

But the most kind of leveraged attribute of your application is actual usage of user data or particular specific aspects of how the application works, maybe some aspects of your harness, which tools do you provide, how the application works, kind of really important bits which are important for your application.

paragraphs.zh: 对，绝对是。我的意思是，我们实际上通常把这看作 web applications 的一种演化模式。你一开始可能是在做原型，或者会先用现成的 off-the-shelf model 跑起一个东西，也许再做一些 prompt engineering（提示工程），弄清楚你的 harness 是怎么工作的。但你这个应用里最有杠杆效应的属性，其实是对用户数据的实际使用，或者应用如何运行的一些非常具体的方面，也可能是 harness 的某些部分、你提供了哪些 tools、应用如何工作——这些真正重要的 bits，才是对你的应用最关键的东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And the right way to capture that, you can do a little bit of that through prompting.
But really, the right way to do this is craft your model to act in your environment.

paragraphs.zh: 而要把这些东西正确地捕捉下来，确实可以通过 prompting（提示）做到一小部分。但真正正确的方式，是把你的 model 打造成能够在你的环境中行动。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Absolutely.
Like, are certain tools the agent calls that it's very hard to succinctly describe exactly the behavior of that tool to the model.
And, you know, with just like post training, we can bake in the optimal way to use those tools.

Like composer, we we do serve a prompt to composer, but I I think the way we are training it, it would work even without a prompt, and it would know what to do just because, like, we are intrinsically pushing the model to, like, the right direction of how it should act throughout our training.

paragraphs.zh: 对，完全如此。比如有些 tools，是 agent 调用的工具，你很难用简洁的方式把这个工具的具体行为准确描述给 model。而通过 post training（后训练），我们就可以把使用这些工具的最优方式直接 bake in（固化）进去。像 composer，我们确实会给 composer 提供一个 prompt，但我觉得按照我们的训练方式，它甚至在没有 prompt 的情况下也能工作，也会知道该做什么。因为我们在整个训练过程中，本质上就是在把 model 往它应当如何行动的正确方向上推。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Basically, there's kinda,

paragraphs.zh: 基本上，这有点像，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: like, upper bound of, like, how far you can get this prompt engineering.
And if you want to craft really great AI products, you have to go through kind of and influencing model behavior.
That's kind of one reason.
I mean, reason number two is what the recommendation is kind of cost trade off or, like, speed trade off.
Like, the way we kind of view it as Fireworks is that that, you know, trying to do optimization.

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，这种 prompt engineering（提示词工程）到底能把效果推到多远，大概是有一个上限的。如果你想打造真正出色的 AI 产品，你就必须经历某种对模型行为进行塑造和影响的过程。这算是一个原因。我觉得第二个原因，本质上是 recommendation（推荐）里的成本权衡，或者说速度权衡。我们在 Fireworks 的看法是，这其实就是在做 optimization（优化）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You had this, like, three-dimensional trade off between quality, speed, and cost.
And you can go quite far, and we are doing it with all the customers.
Initially, we can go quite far with just optimizing infrastructure.
But when you start getting to model training, can really push this trade off much further, and you can get better model at fraction of the cost running much faster.
And, you know, Composer is a great example of

paragraphs.zh: 这里有一个类似三维的权衡：质量、速度和成本。我们现在和所有客户都在做这件事，而且只靠优化基础设施，一开始其实就已经能走得很远了。但当你进一步做到 model training（模型训练）这一层时，就能把这种权衡再往前大幅推进：你可以用一小部分成本，得到更好的模型，而且运行速度还快得多。你知道，Composer 就是一个很好的例子，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Can I push on this a little bit?
I wanna ask if this approach is bitter lesson pills.
And we were we were actually all talking about tab nine on the walk in.
I'm remembering before the LLM era, there were these, like, small specialized coding models.

And one of the things that was, I think, surprising to to a lot of people was as you've scaled up, you know, you scaled up just training on the Internet and a lot a bunch of English texts and other languages.

paragraphs.zh: 我能不能在这点上再追问一下？我想问，这种方法算不算是 bitter lesson（苦涩教训）那一派的思路。其实我们刚才走进来时也一直在聊 tab nine。我记得在 LLM 时代之前，曾经有那种小型、专门化的 coding model（代码模型）。而我觉得让很多人都挺意外的一件事是，随着规模扩大——也就是主要在 Internet 上、以及大量英文文本和其他语言文本上扩展训练——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Actually, the models themselves got inherently better at coding as well.
And so at least the trend line I've seen so far is just like bigger models perform better on everything, including on coding.
Is what you guys are saying does that go against the the grain of the bitter lesson?

paragraphs.zh: 模型本身其实也天然地变得更擅长 coding（编程）了。所以至少就我目前看到的趋势线来说，就是模型越大，几乎在所有事情上表现都更好，coding 也包括在内。那你们现在说的这些，是否有点违背 bitter lesson 的基本方向？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think no. But one one sort of, like, thing to point out is that the big models trained by the labs train on a lot of code as well.
Like, code is one of the main tasks the labs are interested in pushing, and so they don't just generalize to it.
They're a bit specialized as well.
I think for our case, actually, you know, if we believe about the bitter lesson, we are just pushing very hard on the data dimension, and we know that the models inherently have finite capacity.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得没有。不过有一点要指出：这些由 labs 训练出来的大模型，本身也训练了大量代码数据。代码就是 labs 重点推进的主要任务之一，所以它们并不只是泛化到了这件事上，它们其实也有一定程度的专门化。我觉得就我们的情况而言，如果我们相信 bitter lesson，那我们其实只是在 data（数据）这个维度上非常激进地推进，而且我们知道模型天然只有有限的 capacity（容量）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so if we wanna saturate all that capacity, we need to scale data.
And in order to ingest more data, we we need to, like, free up the weights from distractions the model may have.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，如果我们想把这些 capacity 全部吃满，就需要扩展数据规模。而为了摄入更多数据，我们就需要把模型可能存在的一些“干扰项”从 weights（权重）里清出去。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Okay.
Got it.
Super interesting.
Okay.
Let's dig into the training of Composer two.

paragraphs.zh: 好的，明白了。非常有意思。那我们来深入聊聊 Composer two 的训练。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You launched a couple weeks ago, immediately grabbed attention.
Strong benchmark numbers, much lower cost to to run inference on.
What's the short version of how Composer two works and and what you guys did to make it so performant?

paragraphs.zh: 你们是几周前发布的，一出来就立刻引起了关注。benchmark（基准测试）数字很强，inference（推理）运行成本又低得多。简短来说，Composer two 是怎么工作的？你们具体做了什么，才让它性能这么强？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We started from a very strong base, which is Kymi 2.5.
That's like a 1,000,000,000,000 m parameter MOE that's 30 b active.
So very, very sparse actually.
We sort of like looked at the stock and realized there are like two axes.
So mainly Composer one was just pushing on one of these axes, which is reinforcement learning, but composer two pushes in two different axes.

paragraphs.zh: 我们起步于一个非常强的基础模型，也就是 Kymi 2.5。它大概相当于一个 1,000,000,000,000 参数的 MOE，但 active 参数是 30b。所以实际上它非常、非常稀疏。我们算是审视了一下整个情况，意识到这里有两个维度。基本上，Composer one 主要只是沿着其中一个维度推进，也就是 reinforcement learning（强化学习），但 composer two 则在两个不同的维度上同时推进。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: One is continual pre training and the other is reinforcement learning.
So the thing that made composer two very good is pushing in both of these directions.
So we started off the training ground by doing lots of mid training on code tokens, almost sort of pre training scale actually.
And then coming out of that mid training ground, we took the checkpoints and we did very large scale RL on lots of lots of tasks.

paragraphs.zh: 一个是 continual pre training（持续预训练），另一个是 reinforcement learning（强化学习）。所以让 composer two 变得非常强的原因，就是同时在这两个方向上发力。我们的训练起点是先在 code tokens（代码 token）上做了大量 mid training（中期训练），规模其实几乎接近 pre training（预训练）了。然后在完成那一阶段的 mid training 之后，我们拿这些 checkpoints（检查点）去做了大规模的 RL，覆盖了非常非常多的任务。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Okay.

And then the premise here would be because Cursor sits in the middle of so many interesting coding tokens, you actually pretty uniquely have access to data to be able to train at almost pretraining scale.
Yeah.
Why not pretrain your own model then?

paragraphs.zh: 好的。这里的前提应该是，因为 Cursor 处在大量有意思的 coding tokens（编程 token）流的中间位置，所以你们实际上以一种相当独特的方式拿到了数据，因此能够进行接近预训练规模的训练。是的。那为什么不直接预训练你们自己的模型呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We just think about our approach from top down instead of bottom up.

So like, how do we get a model that's useful to users in the least time possible if we were to start from the bottom, of figure out how how we do pre training and then scale it up to mid training and then, okay, now we figured out mid training, we do reinforcement learning, that would take a very long time to get a model out to our users.
By doing it the other way around, we were able to give our useful model to our users in very little time.
So hopefully, you know, like, next composer versions are gonna be our own model instead of basing it off on open source base.

paragraphs.zh: 我们思考方法是自上而下，而不是自下而上。也就是说，如果目标是用尽可能短的时间做出一个对用户有用的模型，那么如果我们从底层开始，先研究怎么做 pre training，再把它扩展到 mid training，然后再说，好，现在 mid training 也搞明白了，我们再做 reinforcement learning，那样要花很长时间才能把模型交付给用户。反过来做的话，我们就能在很短时间内把一个有用的模型提供给用户。所以理想情况下，希望接下来的 composer 版本会是我们自己的模型，而不是基于 open source base（开源基础模型）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And what is the model roughly learning in the kind of mid training step?
What is the model learning in the post training step for you?

paragraphs.zh: 那么，模型在 mid training 这一步里大致学到了什么？对你们来说，它在 post training（后训练）这一步里又学到了什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
So in mid training, it's sort of just kind of learning about libraries of code and learning about specific code patterns that are very common, like just world knowledge as well.
There is like web data there as well.
And this is sort of just creating a wider distribution that then reinforcement learning can sharpen on.

And so during reinforcement learning, you know, the model gets to play directly with the cursor harness, and so it gets to learn about the world the model is gonna live in for the rest of its life, right, in in some way.

paragraphs.zh: 是的。所以在 mid training 阶段，它学到的主要是代码库相关的知识，以及一些非常常见的特定代码模式，也包括一些 world knowledge（世界知识）。里面也有 web data（网页数据）。这一步本质上是在构建一个更宽的分布，后续 reinforcement learning 就可以在这个分布上进一步“磨尖”它。所以到了 reinforcement learning 阶段，模型就能直接和 Cursor harness 交互，因此它能学习自己在接下来“余生”里将要生存的那个世界，对吧，从某种意义上说就是这样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And and so then during reinforcement learning, that's where it learns how to call tools properly, how to navigate its environment, how to write correct code.
Because during mid training, it it learns how to write code.
That doesn't necessarily mean it learns how to write correct code.
We try to train on code that is largely only correct, but the model doesn't actually know how to differentiate between the two.
While in RL, one of the key things that we are doing is we're kind of tuning the feature of the model saying, hey, now you gotta right, correct code all the time.

paragraphs.zh: 然后在 reinforcement learning 过程中，它才真正学会如何正确调用工具、如何在环境中导航、如何写出正确的代码。因为在 mid training 期间，它学会的是“如何写代码”，但这并不一定意味着它学会了“如何写正确的代码”。我们会尽量只用大体上正确的代码来训练，但模型本身其实并不知道该如何区分这两者。而在 RL 里，我们做的一件关键事情，就是在调整模型的特征，相当于在告诉它：现在你必须始终写出正确的代码。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Exactly.

paragraphs.zh: 完全正确。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Interesting.
And is the is the model after mid training, is that similar to the model you guys have on tab auto complete, or is that a different core competency?

paragraphs.zh: 有意思。所以这个模型在经过 mid training 之后，是否和你们用于 tab auto complete（Tab 自动补全）的模型相似，还是说那是另一种不同的核心能力？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, it's yeah.
That think I would put it like that because, like, during mid training, we are just doing the next token prediction, you know, like how well you predict the next token and then the token after that.
So yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我的意思是，对。这个我会这么表述，因为在 mid training（中期训练）阶段，我们其实只是在做 next token prediction（下一个 token 预测），也就是看你能把下一个 token 以及再下一个 token 预测得有多好。所以，对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So why not just post train on your tab on a complete model then?
Why mid trains the different model?

paragraphs.zh: 那为什么不直接在一个完整模型上对你们的 tab 做 post-train（后训练）呢？为什么要对另一个不同的模型做 mid training？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, tab is a very small model because it's like a super low latency model as we want it to be very fast.
So like the core two distinctions about the base models here is that tab is like small and and composer is quite large.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我的意思是，tab 是一个非常小的模型，因为它是那种超低延迟的模型，我们希望它非常快。所以这里这些 base model（基础模型）的两个核心区别是：tab 很小，而 Composer 则相当大。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I see.
I see.
Okay.
So it seems like a lot of the focus of what you guys did for Composer two was this large scale reinforcement learning run.
Can you break that down for us?

paragraphs.zh: 明白了，明白了。好。所以看起来，你们为 Composer two 所做工作的很大一部分重点，是这次大规模 reinforcement learning（强化学习）训练。你能帮我们拆解一下吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, what goes into that, and what are the various hard problems you solve along the way?

paragraphs.zh: 比如，这里面都包含哪些内容？以及在这个过程中，你们解决了哪些不同的难题？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: When you do RL, it's quite different from, like, pre training or retraining, because you're not just trying to predict next token.
You're actually running the entire harness, like, the entire experiment.
You're letting the model act in the environment, see how how it performs for a given rollout.

That's the terminology which is called rollout, and kind of assign it to reward whether it did something correctly or not, which might be some l using LM as a judge or maybe something verifiable, like does this code compile or something like this, which actually means that compared to this regular training, would need a bunch of other components.
Like, you still need large scale training.

paragraphs.zh: 当你做 RL（强化学习）时，它和 pre-training（预训练）或 retraining（再训练）很不一样，因为你不只是想去预测下一个 token。你实际上是在运行整套 harness（执行框架），也就是整个实验。你让模型在环境中行动，观察它在某一次 rollout（采样执行）中的表现如何。这就是所谓的 rollout 这个术语，然后根据它是否正确完成了某件事来赋予 reward（奖励）；这个判断可能是用 LM as a judge（让语言模型充当评审）之类的方法，也可能是某种可验证的标准，比如这段代码能不能编译之类。这实际上意味着，相比常规训练，你会需要一整套额外的组件。当然，你仍然需要大规模训练。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You still need to orchestrate tens of thousands of GPUs to the forward backward propagation, do all the stuff you do in mid training and pre training.
But now you also need to orchestrate a bunch of environments.
You need to run model inference because when you do this rollout, you're effectively running like real Courser session in some sense, right?
So if you have your

paragraphs.zh: 你仍然需要协调成千上万块 GPU 来做 forward/backward propagation（前向/反向传播），完成你在 mid training 和 pre-training 中会做的那些事情。但现在你还需要协调一大批环境。你还需要运行 model inference（模型推理），因为当你进行这种 rollout 时，从某种意义上说，你实际上是在运行一个真实的 Courser session，对吧？所以如果你有你的

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So rollout is like a forward pass.

paragraphs.zh: 所以 rollout 就像一次 forward pass（前向传递）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: No, rollout is basically your entire agent session from Courser, right?
So we basically means it might take something like 50 turns.
Model will take your initial prompt, then decide to call some tools.
You want to execute those tools.
Then model generates a bunch of other code Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 不，rollout 基本上就是你在 Courser 里的整个 agent session，对吧？也就是说，它可能会持续大概 50 个 turn。model 会先接收你的初始 prompt，然后决定调用一些 tools。你需要去执行这些 tools。接着 model 又会生成一堆别的代码。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Kind of entire session which you, when you interact with agent and cursor, right?
You kind of simulate this entire session as a part of your training run.
You get to final rewards and use that signal to now go back to trainer and incorporate it in the model weights.
So you have this kind of very big update loop, which is very heterogeneous, right, because you have all these different components working together.
And now you're trying to orchestrate all of this to work efficiently and work with high throughputs because GPUs are expensive, and you want to get your model trained quickly in an economic fashion.

paragraphs.zh: 可以把它理解为你与 agent 和 cursor 交互时的整个 session，对吧？你会把这个完整 session 作为 training run 的一部分来模拟。你拿到最终 rewards（奖励）后，就用这个 signal（信号）回到 trainer，并把它整合进 model weights（模型权重）里。所以这里会形成一个非常大的 update loop（更新循环），而且它是高度 heterogeneous（异构）的，对吧，因为有很多不同组件在一起协同工作。现在你还要设法把这一切高效地编排起来，并实现高 throughput（吞吐量），因为 GPUs 很贵，而你希望以经济的方式尽快把 model 训练好。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So that's, by by itself, is, like, very interesting kind of problem on intersection of algorithms and infrastructure, because there are a lot of trade offs how you can kind of co optimize and co design the system.
One aspect is kind of people call about, like, a of a pipeline URL.
The idea is basically, okay, you're trying to update this model in steps.
Right?
So you have your current model version, and you are trying to do a bunch of rollouts with it.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这本身就是一个很有意思的问题，处在 algorithms（算法）和 infrastructure（基础设施）的交叉点上，因为在如何对系统进行协同优化和协同设计这件事上，有很多 trade-off（权衡）。其中一个方面，有些人会谈到类似 pipeline URL 这样的概念。它的基本意思是：你在按步骤更新这个 model，对吧？所以你有当前版本的 model，然后你试图用它去做一批 rollouts。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: What does your trainer do while you're doing these rollouts?
Right?
Like, naive approach would say that, okay, now I'm gonna stop my trainer.
I'm gonna do a bunch of sessions, and those sessions might run for, five, ten minutes or even longer if it's, like, longer horizon tasks.
I'm gonna get those outcomes.

paragraphs.zh: 那么当你在做这些 rollouts 的时候，trainer 在干什么呢？对吧？一个很 naive（朴素）的做法会说，好，现在我要先停掉 trainer。然后我去跑一批 sessions，而这些 sessions 可能要跑 5 分钟、10 分钟，如果是更长时程的任务，甚至可能更久。然后我再拿到这些结果。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And now I'm gonna pause my inference.
I mean, I go back to training, trying to do updates.
That's, like, very theoretically, algorithmically robust because you are not precisely simulating everything.
But it's very system inefficient because half of your capacity is sitting idle as time.
So you can do all the clever, like, algorithmic tricks, allowing

paragraphs.zh: 然后我再暂停 inference（推理）。也就是说，我回到 training，尝试做更新。从理论和算法角度看，这样是很稳健的，因为你并不是在精确地把一切都同时模拟起来。但从系统角度看，这样效率很低，因为有一半的 capacity（算力容量）会在一段时间里处于闲置状态。所以你就可以做各种聪明的、偏算法层面的技巧，从而让——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You need to to instead.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 你其实需要改成另一种方式。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You you can like kind of pipeline all of this.
So imagine this as a gigantic, like, factory.
Right?
You have this, like, trainer building, and you have rollouts building.
They're always churning, right?

paragraphs.zh: 你可以把这一切做成一种 pipeline（流水线）。把它想象成一个巨大的工厂，对吧？你有 trainer 这栋楼，也有 rollouts 这栋楼。它们始终都在不停运转，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So rollouts always take latest model version and try to do new sessions and kind of simulate new agent sessions.
And trainer always takes new outcomes as they come and try to compute updates.
So everything is moving along all the time.

The trade off is that why I'm saying that algorithmically it's different, because now by the time you finish some test rollouts in your kind of simulated environment, maybe model weights already updated on some other data.

So you have this kind of staleness, like, delay between how quickly model can learn updates, because by the time you kind of process for some interaction session with the simulated environment, your model base changed.

paragraphs.zh: 所以 rollouts 会不断拿最新版本的 model，去开启新的 sessions，并模拟新的 agent sessions。而 trainer 则会不断接收新出来的 outcomes（结果），并尝试计算 updates（更新）。于是所有东西始终都在持续向前推进。这里的 trade-off 是——这也是为什么我说它在算法上会不一样——因为等你在这个模拟环境里完成某些测试 rollouts 时，model weights 可能已经基于其他数据更新过了。所以这里会出现一种 staleness（陈旧性），也就是 model 学习更新的速度与环境交互处理速度之间存在延迟，因为等你把某个模拟环境中的交互 session 处理完时，model base 已经变了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And that introduces interesting training dynamics.
And there are clever ways how you can address this.
But the flip side of that is that your all your GPUs, all your computers kind of loaded and chime in all the time, which actually you're using more more flops and to your bitter less an example.
Yeah.
You you have, like, higher compute efficiency.

paragraphs.zh: 这就引入了很有意思的训练动态（training dynamics）。而且有一些很巧妙的方法可以处理这个问题。但它的另一面是，你所有的 GPU、所有的计算机基本上一直都在加载并持续参与进来，实际上这意味着你用了更多的 flops（浮点运算量），而且用一个例子来说，代价也更低一些。对。你会获得更高的 compute efficiency（计算效率）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You can get to a better model in smaller amount of time.
Yeah.
Maybe you're losing a few percent from being asynchronous and not doing, like, perfect mathematical updates.
Yeah.
But you way compensate for that by effectively not leaving half your capacity on the table.

paragraphs.zh: 你可以在更短的时间内得到一个更好的模型。对。也许由于 asynchronous（异步）以及没有进行那种完美的数学更新，你会损失几个百分点。对。但你完全可以通过不把一半的算力白白闲置在那里，来远远弥补这一点。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And there are a lot of kind of depths and interest in interaction in that part.

paragraphs.zh: 而且这部分里还有很多层次，以及很多值得关注的交互。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And we're very serious about performance at Cursor because unlike the big labs, you know, we have tens of thousands of GPUs, not millions.
And so, yeah, we do all sorts of tricks to make get the most out of GPU.
Like, we train in production with f before even.
We work with fireworks to to, like, push on inference as well.
Because the thing about RL infrastructure is just like it's just inherently more complex than pretraining because you need all the pretraining infrastructure.

paragraphs.zh: 我们在 Cursor 对性能是非常认真的，因为不像那些大型实验室，你知道，我们只有几万块 GPU，不是几百万块。所以，对，我们会用各种技巧来尽可能压榨 GPU 的性能。比如，我们甚至会在 production（生产环境）里提前用 F 进行训练。我们也和 fireworks 合作，去推动 inference（推理）这部分的表现。因为 RL（强化学习）基础设施的问题在于，它天生就比 pretraining（预训练）更复杂，因为你首先需要整套预训练基础设施。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's just like one of the requirements.
Then you need all the infrastructure to run these environments that have to mimic as closely as possible what a user as a computer would look like.

And it's very important as closely as possible because sometimes the model can actually figure out when it's being run-in like a fake environment or not a real one, and it has like different behaviors during RL than in production.

paragraphs.zh: 那还只是其中一个要求。然后你还需要整套基础设施来运行这些 environment（环境）；这些环境必须尽可能逼真地模拟一个用户在电脑上实际会是什么样子。这里“尽可能逼真”非常重要，因为有时候模型其实能分辨出自己是不是在一个 fake environment（伪造环境）里运行，而不是真实环境；这样一来，它在 RL 训练期间的行为就可能和在 production 里的行为不一样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Are you seeing it being conscious that it's being it's in a fake environment, it starts being behaving differently?

paragraphs.zh: 你是说，你们观察到它会意识到自己处在一个 fake environment 里，于是开始表现得不一样吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
Interesting.

paragraphs.zh: 是的，是的。有意思。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Like, it's like, oh, I'm in a fake environment.
I've learned a few tricks to, like, get a better reward in this environment, and let me try them out.

paragraphs.zh: 就像它会觉得，“哦，我现在在一个 fake environment 里。我学会了一些小技巧，可以在这个环境里拿到更高的 reward（奖励），那我来试试看。”

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Models love to cheat.
ARAL is really good at encouraging cheating.

paragraphs.zh: 模型很喜欢作弊。ARAL 非常擅长鼓励作弊。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
And then we need a really efficient inference.
So this is really important.
So there is like actually this kind of myth that during GARAL you spend more, way more inference flops than training flops.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对。然后我们还需要非常高效的 inference（推理）。所以这一点非常重要。其实有一种说法，像是在 GARAL 期间，你花在 inference 上的 flops 会比 training（训练）flops 多很多。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: This is sort of like just because the open source inference engines are very unoptimized instead of actually being a property of RL.
Roughly the same ratio is kind of the same.
In theory, if you push the GPUs to the maximum, you should have one third of your training GPUs allocated to inference.
Right?
Because training is effectively three forward passes.

paragraphs.zh: 这在某种程度上只是因为开源 inference engine（推理引擎）优化得很差，而并不是 RL（强化学习）本身的属性。大致来说，这个比例其实差不多。理论上，如果你把 GPUs 压到最大利用率，你应该把训练用 GPU 数量的三分之一分配给 inference。对吧？因为 training 实际上相当于三次 forward pass（前向传播）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You have the forward pass, you have the data gradient, the weight gradient.
While if you really hit the critical batch size on inference, you should only have a single forward password of flops.

paragraphs.zh: 你有一次 forward pass，还有 data gradient（数据梯度）和 weight gradient（权重梯度）。而如果你在 inference 时真的打到了 critical batch size（临界批大小），那你理论上只需要一次前向计算对应的 flops。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So that's why you guys use fireworks instead of using an open inference engine?

paragraphs.zh: 所以这就是你们使用 fireworks、而不是开源推理引擎的原因吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
I mean, the other alternative is we would build one in house, but you know, we have finite engineers like everybody else.
We would, like, prefer to have engineers make training more efficient and more precise rather than, like, spin up like inference effort.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我的意思是，另一个选择是我们自己内部做一个，但你知道，和其他人一样，我们的工程师数量也是有限的。我们更希望让工程师去把 training 做得更高效、更精确，而不是另外再拉起一套 inference 方面的工作。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Okay.
That's super hardcore.
What about thinking you mentioned in your technical paper paper that you were doing this in a kind of globally distributed way.
Why globally distributed?
And then what makes that hard?

paragraphs.zh: 好的。这很硬核。那关于 thinking（思维链/思考）呢？你在技术论文里提到，你们是以一种全球分布式的方式来做这件事。为什么要全球分布式？另外，这件事难在哪里？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, there are various reasons.
One, you know, like this very large contiguous clusters are harder to find in the market.
And so what we can do instead is we have one cluster that's gonna run all of training.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对。这个有几个原因。首先，你知道，这种非常大的、连续成片的 cluster（集群）在市场上更难找到。所以我们可以改成这样做：我们有一个 cluster 来跑全部的 training。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You know, we can't do global training cluster.
But then the inference component of reinforcement learning, we can globally distribute that across small clusters all over the world.
So I think for the composer to run, we use the four clusters in total that were all over the world, very far away from each other.
And we even used some of our production traffic when it was least used.
So like we had the Composer 1.5, the previous model served, and when it was least used by people, we just grabbed some inference GPUs and we put them to speed up training.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，我们没法做全球性的 training cluster（训练集群）。但在 reinforcement learning（强化学习）的 inference（推理）部分，我们可以把它分布到全球各地的小型 cluster（集群）上。所以我认为，为了让 Composer 运行，我们总共用了四个分布在世界各地、彼此相距很远的 cluster。我们甚至还用了部分 production traffic（生产流量）中的资源，在它使用率最低的时候调配出来。比如当 Composer 1.5，也就是前一个模型还在提供服务时，在用户使用最少的时段，我们就抽出一些用于 inference 的 GPU，把它们拿来加速训练。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so we can do these sort of things and sort of easily scale up our training ground without having one large contiguous cluster.
And the thing that enables it, maybe Vidima can talk more I mean,

paragraphs.zh: 所以我们可以做这类事情，而且不需要一个大型、连续的一体化 cluster，就能相对轻松地扩展我们的 training ground（训练资源池）。而让这成为可能的原因，也许 Vidima 可以多讲一点，我的意思是，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: kinda like to to reframeulate what Federico said is basically, RL training is, like, very heterogeneous.
Right?
And by leveraging heterogeneity, how different components like, what infrastructures they need, you can actually drive efficiency.
And you see this pattern kind of across the board everywhere.
Specifically for for training, you have all these, like, highly interconnected clusters.

paragraphs.zh: 有点像是换个方式表述 Federico 说的话：RL training（强化学习训练）本质上是非常 heterogeneous（异构）的，对吧？而通过利用这种异构性，也就是不同组件、不同基础设施需求之间的差异，你实际上可以把效率提上去。你会看到这种模式几乎到处都是。具体到训练，你会有这些高度互联的 cluster。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You need high speed network, kind of need to work in lockstep.
So those clusters are expensive.
Right?
And actually, it's really hard to find big ones.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 你需要高速网络，基本上还需要它们步调一致地协同工作。所以这类 cluster 很贵，对吧？而且实际上，要找到很大的这种 cluster 也非常难，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Basically, at the scale at which Composer was trained, finding like 2x larger cluster is like significantly harder than finding the current size one.
And that's why if you can disaggregate these components and put them on different places, one, you don't need to find such a big cluster.
Two, you can actually find different trade offs of hardware.
Because for inference, you don't need that of wide interconnect.
You can have smaller groups of GPUs interconnected together.

paragraphs.zh: 基本上，以训练 Composer 时的那个规模来看，想找到一个大 2 倍的 cluster，要比找到当前这个规模的 cluster 难得多。这就是为什么，如果你能把这些组件拆分开、放到不同地方，一来你不需要去找那么大的 cluster，二来你还可以针对硬件做不同的 trade-off（权衡）。因为对 inference 来说，你不需要那么宽的 interconnect（互连带宽）。你可以只让较小规模的 GPU 组彼此互连。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You can have heterogeneous types of GPUs.
You can have different generations of GPUs.
You can kind of play all these games games optimization.
And finally, like inference, it's much easier to scale up and down as you go.
And, yeah, it's very conventional.

paragraphs.zh: 你可以用 heterogeneous types of GPUs（异构类型的 GPU），也可以混用不同代际的 GPU，还可以做各种这类优化上的“操作”。最后，inference 在运行过程中也更容易按需扩容和缩容。对，就是这样，这其实是非常常规的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Like, when you have off peak hours, you can view all your kind of inference pool as one set of GPUs.
Serving production traffic for real users or serving simulated environments for RL purposes and kind of balance between this.
Of course, it's a very interesting systems problem.
So you can mention, like, the Kymi model is, like, one one terabyte.
Training step takes somewhere between, like, five to fifteen minutes.

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，在 off-peak hours（低峰时段），你可以把整个 inference pool（推理资源池）看作一组统一的 GPU：它们要么服务真实用户的 production traffic，要么服务 RL 目的下的 simulated environments（模拟环境），然后在这两者之间做平衡。当然，这本身就是一个非常有意思的 systems problem（系统问题）。再比如你可以提到，Kymi model 大约有 one terabyte（一 TB）大小，而一次 training step（训练步）大概需要五到十五分钟。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So it basically means, like, every, like, every five to ten minutes, you are producing, like, one terabyte new snapshot of weights.
So the question is, like, how are you gonna ship it to a different cluster on this other side of the world very efficiently.
Right?
And you want to, like, do it quickly because remember, you don't want to get this staleness to get out of hand.
So I think that was probably one Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这基本意味着，大约每五到十分钟，你就会产出一个新的、大小约为一 TB 的 weights（权重）快照。于是问题就变成：你要怎样才能非常高效地把它传送到地球另一端的另一个 cluster？对吧？而且你会想尽快完成，因为别忘了，你不希望这种 staleness（陈旧性、延迟）失控。所以我觉得这大概就是其中一个关键问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: The the kind of the the most fun part which we figured out together is that despite, you know, full full model being like one terabyte, not all the weights change every step.
Right?
Because RL does a lot of very, like, precise adjustments, especially as the training going on.
So actually, there are very kind of regular patterns in, like, which subset of weights gets changed.
Maybe not all of them change every time.

paragraphs.zh: 我们一起想明白的、也是最有意思的部分是：尽管完整模型（full model）大概有 1 TB，但并不是每一步都会有所有权重（weights）发生变化，对吧？因为 RL 会做很多非常精细的调整，尤其是在训练持续进行时更是如此。所以实际上，哪些权重子集会被改动，往往会呈现出相当规律的模式；也就是说，也许并不是每次所有权重都会变化。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So if you were to look at, like, how my model changes within one training step, like, after ten minutes, there is relatively small delta between those.
You can write write a compression algorithm, which basically leverages this property.
And now you end up with kinda like database systems problem, which is, okay.
I have my delta, and I just want to, like, ship it across across the world.
My delta might be, like, 20 times smaller than what ship in the full model is.

paragraphs.zh: 所以如果你去看我的模型在一个训练步（training step）内部是怎么变化的，比如说，隔了十分钟之后，两者之间的 delta（增量差异）其实相对很小。你就可以写一种压缩算法（compression algorithm），本质上就是利用这个性质。这样一来，你面对的就有点像是一个 database systems（数据库系统）问题：好，我有这个 delta，我只想把它传到世界另一端。这个 delta 可能比传输完整模型小 20 倍左右。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And that and that makes it practical.
But, of course, now you need to build all this kind of machinery from storage systems of full snapshots and deltas and recovery and reconciliation, etcetera.
We were able to build it kind of in lossless fashion.
Basically means that you always end up with bit equivalent model on the other side.
So you don't need to worry about any mass aspects of this, and you can do it really fast too.

paragraphs.zh: 这就让它变得实用了。当然，随之而来的是，你需要构建整套机制：包括完整快照（full snapshots）、delta、恢复（recovery）、一致性校对（reconciliation）等等，基本上都是存储系统（storage systems）里的那套东西。我们能够以一种无损（lossless）的方式把它做出来。基本意思就是：另一端最终拿到的模型在 bit 级别是完全等价的。因此你不需要担心这件事在数学层面会有什么偏差，而且它也可以做得非常快。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You can you you can do it under, you know, under a few minutes.
Even in the worst conditions, usually, it's under a minute.
And most importantly, you, like, pause only for, like, maybe thirty seconds to swap the weights in your actual inference server.

paragraphs.zh: 你可以在几分钟以内完成这件事。即使在最差的条件下，通常也不到一分钟。最重要的是，你在实际的 inference server（推理服务器）里替换权重时，只需要暂停大概 30 秒。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: To, like, fully, like, saturated the band the egress of the cluster by, like, sharding the upload and the download as well.
So you

paragraphs.zh: 另外，还可以通过把上传和下载也做 sharding（分片），来尽可能把集群的出口带宽（egress）跑满。这样你就

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: can do all these, like, system tricks to print the stand down.
It is it is quite a few complexity, but you can kind of abstract it out and just make it work great.
Like, it doesn't interfere with your training algorithm.
And on the flip side, you have this kind of power to disaggregate, to leverage other clusters to do all that.
And that kind of goes against kind of conventional wisdom of how you should do RL infrastructure because conventional wisdom is like you okay.

paragraphs.zh: 可以用各种系统层面的技巧把停机时间压下来。这确实带来不少复杂性，但你可以把这些复杂性抽象掉，让它整体运行得很好。比如说，它不会干扰你的训练算法。反过来，你就获得了这种解耦（disaggregate）的能力，可以利用其他集群来完成这些工作。而这在某种程度上是和关于 RL 基础设施（infrastructure）的传统看法相违背的，因为传统看法会觉得：好吧，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You're gonna have this really huge one cluster connected with RDMA, and it's gonna be very expensive, and you're gonna probably spend you know, maybe you're gonna allocate, like, one third to training and two thirds to inference.
And sure, if you have very expensive network, it's much easier to copy this one terabyte quickly.
But now we have, like, three times larger cluster.
Now if your inference engine is more optimized, then maybe you you're gonna save one third of that cluster in terms of GPUs anyway, because you're just more efficient.
And you can take, you know, half of this cluster somewhere else in a maybe cheaper hardware in a different region.

paragraphs.zh: 你需要一个通过 RDMA 连接起来的超大单一集群，这会非常昂贵，而且你大概会把其中三分之一分配给训练、三分之二分配给推理。当然，如果你的网络非常昂贵且性能很强，那要快速复制这 1 TB 的模型确实会容易得多。但现在我们可以用一个大 3 倍的集群。再加上，如果你的 inference engine（推理引擎）优化得更好，那你本来在这个集群里用在 GPU 上的资源，可能本身就能省下三分之一，因为你的效率更高。并且你还可以把这个集群里的一半放到别的地方，比如放在另一个区域、用更便宜的硬件。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So your cost comes down quite a bit.

paragraphs.zh: 这样一来，你的成本就会降下来很多。

And so it's just like a it's an amazing, amazing system you guys have called.

paragraphs.zh: 我很喜欢你们在描述这件事时一直笑得合不拢嘴，因为这就像——它太难了，而这简直是 systems engineer（系统工程师）的梦想，对吧？所以这就像一个你们做出来的、非常非常惊人的系统。

What about I mean, you went to the beginning that Kimi is a very large sparse MOE model.
Does that make the RL run tricky in any way?

paragraphs.zh: 看起来你们确实一起花了很长很长时间。那我想问的是，你前面提到过 Kimi 是一个非常大的稀疏 MOE model（混合专家模型），这会不会让 RL（强化学习）的运行在某些方面变得棘手？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Mhmm.
Yep.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: How so?

paragraphs.zh: 具体怎么说？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, when you do inference, you're essentially doing like a forward pass.
It's just kinda like autoregressive.
And in this forward pass, it produces like log probabilities of like the tokens it it has sampled.
When we ship back the like generations of the model to the trainer, we have to rerun that forward pass because as we mentioned, we are doing asynchronous training.
So the model that has produced the pass may have been like actually a few steps behind what the trainer is at.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，当你做 inference（推理）时，本质上你是在做一次 forward pass（前向传播）。它基本上就是 autoregressive（自回归）的。在这次 forward pass 里，它会产出它所采样出的 token 的 log probabilities（对数概率）。当我们把模型生成的这些 generations（生成结果）回传给 trainer（训练器）时，我们必须重新跑一遍那个 forward pass，因为正如我们提到的，我们做的是 asynchronous training（异步训练）。所以，产生这次结果的模型，相对于 trainer 当前所在的状态，实际上可能已经落后了好几步。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so we have to rerun that forward pass and reproduce log probabilities.
Now the problem is in theory, this log probability should be exactly the same if it's the same model version.
But even with the same model version, you get slightly or sometimes very different log probability values for the same tokens.
So this is often called what, like a numerical mismatch for inference.
You hear this about all the time these days.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我们必须重新跑那次 forward pass，并重新得到 log probabilities。现在问题在于，理论上，如果是同一个模型版本，这个 log probability 应该是完全一样的。但即使是同一个模型版本，对于同样的 token，你也会得到略有不同、有时甚至差别很大的 log probability 数值。所以这通常被称为什么——比如说，inference（推理）中的 numerical mismatch（数值不一致）。这类问题你现在经常会听到。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: For mixture

paragraphs.zh: 对于 mixture

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Why is that?
Why does that happen?

paragraphs.zh: 为什么会这样？为什么会发生这种情况？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: I mean, primarily because, like, fundamentally floating point arithmetic Yes.
Which is doing this is nondeterministic.
Yep.
So if you Sorry.

paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，主要原因在于，从根本上说，执行这一过程的 floating point arithmetic（浮点运算）是 nondeterministic（非确定性的）。对。所以如果你——抱歉。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Floating point arithmetic is nondeterministic.

paragraphs.zh: 浮点运算是非确定性的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So, you know, we learned this called it, like, you a plus b plus c.
Right?
And, like, c plus b plus a, it's gonna be the same result.
If you're doing this with integers, with whole numbers on the computer, that's gonna be always true.
Yep.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，你知道，我们学过这个，可以把它叫作 a plus b plus c。对吧？像 c plus b plus a，结果应该是一样的。如果你在计算机上用整数、也就是 whole numbers 来做，这一点始终都成立。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: If you're going to do it with floating point numbers, which are actually like approximate approximation numbers, have this like mantissa and exponents, etcetera.
A plus b plus c and c plus b plus a is gonna give you like different results or even like a plus b and b.
So basically, fundamentally, it's accumulation order of like all these operations which models do is basically like multiplications and additions.
And like addition order matters to your final result.
It's all like small differences, but they get amplified through like Exactly.

paragraphs.zh: 但如果你用的是 floating point numbers（浮点数），它们其实更像是近似数，有 mantissa（尾数）和 exponent（指数）之类的东西，那么 a plus b plus c 和 c plus b plus a 就会得到不同的结果，甚至像 a plus b 和 b——所以本质上，问题在于所有这些运算的 accumulation order（累积顺序）；而模型做的基本上就是乘法和加法。加法的顺序会影响你的最终结果。起初都只是些很小的差异，但它们会在——没错——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Millions and billions of operations.
So when you do inference models, usually it doesn't matter that much because you pre train your model.
You're actually pretty robust.
If you, like, flip some bits, it's still gonna produce you, like, good results.
Your benchmarks are not gonna change.

paragraphs.zh: 数百万、数十亿次运算中被放大。所以当你做 inference（推理）模型时，通常这件事影响没那么大，因为你的模型已经 pre train（预训练）好了。它其实相当 robust（鲁棒）；就算你翻转一些 bit（位），它通常仍然会给出不错的结果。你的 benchmarks（基准测试）也不会变化。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: But RL in particular, because you are using this very, very, like, weak signal to teach the model, the noise from this numerical differences can make or break your training.
And that's, like, particularly important.
And it it again, it's a interesting intersection between, like, algorithmic and systems part because, you know, you can write a beautiful mess, and it just doesn't work in practice.
There are ways how you can drive this difference to pretty much zero.
There are always, like, batch invariant ways you can basically, you can be very, very careful and write all your GPU kernels, so they always add numbers in the same order.

paragraphs.zh: 但 RL（强化学习）尤其不同，因为你是在用一种非常非常弱的 signal（信号）来教模型，数值差异带来的噪声就可能决定训练成败。这一点特别重要。而且，这又是 algorithmic（算法）部分和 systems（系统）部分一个很有意思的交叉点，因为你可以写出一套很漂亮的东西，但它在实践中就是跑不起来。其实有一些办法，可以把这种差异基本压到接近于零。总会有一些 batch invariant（批次不变）的做法；本质上，你可以非常非常小心地编写所有 GPU kernels（GPU 内核），让它们始终以同样的顺序相加。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So you always do, like, a plus b plus c and not a different order.
It's possible, but that's always has, like, trade offs.
Right?
Basically, your, like, your system becomes maybe, two x or three x slower.
Again, it becomes an interesting trade off.

paragraphs.zh: 这样你就总是在做 a plus b plus c，而不是用别的顺序。这是可行的，但总会有 trade-offs（权衡）。对吧？基本上，你的系统可能就会变慢到两倍或三倍。这又变成了一个很有意思的权衡。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Like, okay.
What is the 10% of slowdown which we can take?
Or in fact, it's actually perfect.
Few percent of slowdown we can take to address 90% of this difference.
That's, you know, the right trade off, which, you know, we we find together through through iteration.

paragraphs.zh: 比如，好的，我们能接受的 10% 的 slowdown（降速）是多少？或者其实，情况已经相当完美了。我们只需要接受几个百分点的 slowdown，就能解决其中 90% 的差异。这就是那个合适的 trade-off（权衡），而这个权衡是我们通过反复 iteration（迭代）一起找到的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And you mentioned that particularly for MOEs and SPARSD is hard.
The reason for that is that, like, the way MOEs work is that you will take your activations at every layer, and you would run it through gating layer, which basically decides, okay.
For this token, I'm gonna run out of three eighty four experts.
I'm gonna run this eight.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 你还提到，尤其对于 MOEs 和 SPARSD，这件事很难。原因在于，MOEs 的工作方式是：你会在每一层取出 activations（激活值），然后让它通过一个 gating layer（门控层）；这个层基本上会决定：好，对于这个 token，我在 384 个 experts（专家）里，要运行其中这 8 个，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So it's gonna do, like, some mess in, like, top eight scores.
Those eight experts gonna be activated.
Other ones will not be activated for this token.
This operation amplifies your small numerical differences quite a bit because maybe your hidden states were, like, difference by, like, fifth digit after dot.
Doesn't really matter.

paragraphs.zh: 所以它会根据 top 8 scores（前 8 个分数）做一些排序之类的操作。那 8 个 expert 会被激活，其他的则不会为这个 token 被激活。这个操作会相当明显地放大小的数值差异，因为也许你的 hidden states（隐藏状态）只是在小数点后第五位上有一点差别，其实本来并不重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: But this difference made it so you picked expert number seven versus expert number nine as a kind of as a cutoff.
And suddenly, you went and, like, activated totally different part of the model, and your difference got amplified quite a bit.
And my models, by definition, are, like, very more sensitive to this mismatch, again, when you do inference or when you do kind of regular load, it usually doesn't matter as a average out.
But now if you're trying to base this model learn, this difference is huge because your inference activated expert number seven.
Now in your training, you are trying to update expert number nine, which didn't even contribute to that during inference.

paragraphs.zh: 但这个差别会让你在临界点上选中了 expert number seven，而不是 expert number nine。结果一下子你就激活了模型里完全不同的一部分，于是这个差异被大幅放大了。而 MOE models（混合专家模型）按定义来说，对这种 mismatch（不匹配）会更敏感。再说一次，如果你是在做 inference（推理），或者做常规负载，这通常问题不大，因为平均下来会被抹平。但如果你想让这个模型基于这些结果去学习，这个差异就非常大了，因为你的 inference 激活的是 expert number seven，而训练时你却在尝试更新 expert number nine——可它在 inference 期间甚至根本没有参与贡献。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So were you guys handwriting GPU kernels then to help get around this

paragraphs.zh: 所以你们当时是在手写 GPU kernels（GPU 内核）来绕开这个问题吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Yes.
So you can, again, you can address all of this through GPU kernels, and there's always trade off.
Yeah.
Specifically for you can do this interesting trick, which people call a router replay.
But basically, can have your inference just pass extra information to training and say that, hey, I activated expert seven for this token.

paragraphs.zh: 是的。所以这些问题确实都可以通过 GPU kernels 来处理，而且这里面永远都有 trade-off。对。特别是，你可以用一个很有意思的技巧，人们把它叫作 router replay。不过本质上，就是让 inference 给 training 多传一点额外信息，说：嘿，这个 token 我激活的是 expert seven。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: This very small bit piece of information is just one integer saying that, like, okay, this is the expert that you activated.
So trainer can be aligned with that.

And a lot of this numerical alignment is basically doing tricks like that, matching quantization levels, matching matching kernels, etcetera, to drive the divergence between training inference implementation down.

And that makes huge difference in between, you know, your run may be diverging completely or being, you know, multiplex less compute efficient because you'll need much more data to address to this mismatch.

paragraphs.zh: 这一小段信息其实只是一个整数，用来说明：好，你激活的是这个 expert。这样 trainer（训练器）就可以和它对齐。很多这种数值对齐，本质上就是在做这类技巧，比如匹配 quantization levels（量化级别）、匹配 kernels，等等，从而尽量降低 training 和 inference 实现之间的 divergence（偏离）。而这会带来巨大的差别：你的 run（训练运行）可能会完全发散，或者 compute efficient（计算效率）低很多倍，因为你需要更多数据来弥补这种 mismatch。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I'd love to maybe chat a little bit more about the RL kind of recipe.
Can you say a word about the reward signal you're using?
Is it it's like or you can't.
Okay.
Can't say.

paragraphs.zh: 我很想再多聊一点 RL（强化学习）这套 recipe（方法配方）。你能简单说说你们在用的 reward signal（奖励信号）吗？是那种……还是说你不能讲。好，不能讲。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Got it.
Top secret stuff.
Top secret stuff.
Okay.
That makes sense.

paragraphs.zh: 明白了。最高机密。最高机密。好吧。这就说得通了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, seems like this is a almost like the equivalent of learning in sim.
This is simulated rollouts versus, like, you have so much actual user data that you could be learning on.
Why not just do RL on your your actual user data and your actual user harness versus doing this in sim?

paragraphs.zh: 这听起来有点像是在 sim（模拟环境）里学习的对应物。也就是 simulated rollouts（模拟 rollout），而不是基于你们已经拥有的大量真实用户数据来学习。既然你们有这么多真实用户数据，也有真实的 user harness（用户测试/交互框架），为什么不直接在真实用户数据上做 RL（强化学习），而要在 sim 里做呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
We're also doing that.
So that's what we call a real time RL.

paragraphs.zh: 对，我们也在这么做。所以我们把那叫作 real-time RL（实时强化学习）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Okay.

paragraphs.zh: 好的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And we use the same technology to do like the inference with sync with like fireworks to do this.
We find like user signals where the user was happy or sad about a particular model generation, and we're able to update that model live.
And so then ship a new version of the model continuously every few hours.
We're working on decreasing that time.
Actually, at some point, we'll have to increase that time because as the horizon of the model gets longer and longer, we'll have to re extend that time.

paragraphs.zh: 我们也用同样的技术来做 inference（推理），并和 fireworks 保持同步来完成这件事。我们会找到一些 user signals（用户信号），比如用户对某次特定的模型生成结果是满意还是不满意，然后我们就能实时更新那个模型。这样一来，我们每隔几个小时就能持续发布一个新版本的模型。我们现在还在努力缩短这个时间。实际上，到某个阶段我们又得把这个时间拉长，因为随着模型的 horizon（时域/规划跨度）变得越来越长，我们将不得不重新延长这段时间。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's like an interesting play.
Like, right now, we are trying to decrease the time for stability because we were figuring out the right hyper parameters.
And then after we have figured it out, we have to re extend it again just because we wanna lengthen the horizon of these models.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 这算是一个挺有意思的策略。比如说，现在为了稳定性，我们在努力缩短这个时间，因为我们当时还在摸索合适的 hyper parameters（超参数）。但等这些都确定下来之后，我们又得把它重新拉长，因为我们想让这些模型的 horizon（时域/规划跨度）变得更长。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Do you need to do any of the kinda like pretraining simulated RL?
You have so much actual user data.
I imagine that's just like much more valuable to to train and tune on.
Like, why not just go straight to the online RL step?
Why why do you have to do the the offline RL?

paragraphs.zh: 你们还需要做那种有点像 pretraining（预训练）阶段的 simulated RL（模拟强化学习）吗？你们已经有这么多真实用户数据了。我会觉得那些数据拿来训练和调优应该价值高得多。那为什么不直接进入 online RL（在线强化学习）这一步？为什么还非得做 offline RL（离线强化学习）呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: The online RL currently is pretty inefficient.
We suffer from this problem that the GPUs are offline for a long time, essentially.

paragraphs.zh: 目前 online RL 的效率还是相当低。我们遇到的一个问题是，GPU 本质上会有很长时间处于 offline（空闲/未被有效利用）状态。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And beside that, there is also like different trade offs Yeah.
Both in terms of efficiency and user experience.
Yeah.
If you do simulation, you actually do multiple rollouts from the same prompt.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 除此之外，还涉及不同的 trade-offs（权衡）。对，无论是在效率上还是在用户体验上都是这样。对。如果你做 simulation（模拟），你实际上可以对同一个 prompt（提示词）跑多次 rollout（采样轨迹），对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You effectively take a task, and you ask a model to do 16 tries at a task, like 128 tries on task, like different rollouts from, say, prompt.
Some of them are gonna go well.
Some of them are not going well.
And by doing it multiple rollouts in parallel, you are able to get much more precise signal.
Maybe, like, you know, maybe model is very good and it does it well 90% of the time.

paragraphs.zh: 你基本上是拿到一个任务，然后让一个 model 对这个任务尝试 16 次，比如 128 次，像是从同一个 prompt 出发跑出不同的 rollout。有些会做得很好，有些则不会。通过并行做多次 rollout，你就能得到精确得多的 signal。也许，比如说，model 很强，它在 90% 的情况下都能做对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Maybe it's not very good.
Losses like GROPO, like group group policy gradient, like, kind of work by doing multiple rollouts at the same time.
If you're doing online, you have only one rollout coming back.
And so so trade offs of, like, how you do it algorithmically are different.
And most importantly, if your simulated rollout goes wrong, it's not it's not too bad.

paragraphs.zh: 也可能它没那么强。像 GROPO 这样的 loss——比如 group group policy gradient——大致就是通过同时做多个 rollout 来起作用的。如果你做的是 online，你只会拿回一个 rollout。所以在算法上怎么做，这里面的 trade-off 就不一样了。最重要的是，如果你的模拟 rollout 出错了，其实也不算太糟。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Right?
I mean, you just, you know, maybe spend some time on the view.
If it's an actual user, you have much higher, like, minimum bar on this.
Because effectively, you're doing 80 tests.
Right?

sentences.text: That's how I know I'm asking the right questions.

It seems like the whole field is pushing towards making like, long horizon agents, agents that can work for for a long period of time, uninterrupted, and generally not failing.
I love that meter scaling charts.
What goes into into the RL process to try to get the agent to run for longer?

paragraphs.zh: 这就是我知道自己问对问题的方式。你提到 rollout 一次会持续几分钟。看起来整个领域都在推动打造那种 long-horizon agents，也就是能长时间持续工作、不被打断、而且总体上不出错的 agent。我很喜欢那些 meter scaling 图表。为了让 agent 能跑得更久，RL 过程里都要加入哪些东西？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Several things.
So one problem about sort of like reinforcement learning is that the longer the trajectory is, the harder it it is to do credit assignment.
So you can imagine like we are giving thumbs up, down at the bundle, right at the end of its work.
And sort of, like, to simplify the problem is, like, the model asks itself, okay, where did I do right and where did I do wrong?
That's basically the the the problem called gray assignment.

paragraphs.zh: 有几件事。所以，强化学习的一个问题是，trajectory（轨迹）越长，credit assignment（归因分配）就越难。你可以想象，我们是在它完成整包工作、走到最后时，才给一个点赞或点踩。为了简化地说，就是模型会问自己：我到底哪里做对了，哪里做错了？这基本上就是那个叫作 gray assignment 的问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It gets harder as this gets longer, so you have to do a bunch of tricks there.
The other problem is just like you run out of space.
Right?
Like these models have a finite context window, and at some point, they're gonna reach that.
So actually, the way we solve this at cursor is we put compaction inside the RAL loop.

paragraphs.zh: 这个过程越长，问题就越难，所以你必须在那里用很多技巧。另一个问题就是上下文空间会耗尽。对吧？这些模型的 context window（上下文窗口）是有限的，到了某个点，它们总会碰到上限。所以实际上，我们在 cursor 里的解决方式，是把 compaction（压缩）放进 ARAL loop 里面。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So we call this self summarization.
So during reinforcement learning, the agent actually learns how to continue and go on forever.
So in practice, our model is like a 200,000 context window model, but in reality, it can go on for millions of tokens.
And just because of this ability that it can summarize its work and then take that summary to restart its context window while still trying to accomplish the task.
And through ARAL, because ARAL pushes the model to do things correctly towards the goal.

paragraphs.zh: 我们把这个叫作 self summarization（自我总结）。也就是说，在强化学习过程中，agent 实际上学会了如何持续下去、近乎无限地运行。因此在实践中，我们的模型表面上是一个 200,000 context window 的模型，但实际上它可以持续到数百万 tokens。原因就在于它具备这种能力：能先总结自己的工作，再拿着这份总结去重启自己的 context window，同时继续努力完成任务。而通过 ARAL，因为 ARAL 会推动模型朝着目标把事情做对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: At the same time, jointly, we are training the model to produce a good summary, and then we're training the model to listen to that summary very well at the same time.
And so this is kind of like a continuation to reasoning almost, I feel like.

paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，我们在联合地做两件事：一方面训练模型生成好的 summary（摘要），另一方面也同时训练模型去很好地理解这个 summary。所以我觉得，这几乎有点像是对 reasoning（推理）的一种延续。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: I find it fascinating because, I mean, usually context management is considered like part of the hardness.
Right?
In this case, you effectively co optimizing like how part of the harness and, like, model itself work together and throwing all of that into optimization loop.
And we've seen this again and again in AI, that, like, the more you throw computers a problem, the more you can solve this problem end to end.
The magic of computing better lesson works, and you get much better system which can work together.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这很有意思，因为通常 context management（上下文管理）会被看作问题难点的一部分，对吧？但在这个案例里，你实际上是在协同优化：既优化 harness（执行框架/外部控制层）的一部分，也优化模型本身如何一起工作，并把这一切都放进同一个 optimization loop（优化循环）里。我们在 AI 里已经一次又一次看到这一点：你越是把问题整体交给计算机，就越能以端到端的方式解决这个问题。计算能力带来的那种神奇效应确实成立，最后你就能得到一个彼此配合得更好的系统。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Totally.
Totally.
Do you think every company is gonna be their own harnesses?
Like, do you think that every company has the same shape of problem as Cursor?

paragraphs.zh: 完全同意，完全同意。你觉得每家公司都会有自己的 harnesses（执行框架）吗？比如说，你觉得每家公司面对的问题形态，都和 Cursor 是一样的吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: If they are using AI and they're, like, producing of tokens and they have a product to optimize against, I think it's the right move and the right direction to train models.

paragraphs.zh: 如果他们在使用 AI，而且确实在产生大量 token（词元），同时也有一个可以据此优化的产品目标，那我觉得训练模型就是正确的做法，也是正确的方向。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Interesting.
Interesting.
And so it seems like most of the reinforcement learning you guys did then was on the harness slash tool use part rather than on the get good at completing the next token for code.

paragraphs.zh: 对，对。有意思，有意思。所以看起来，你们当时做的大多数 reinforcement learning（强化学习），主要是用在 harness / tool use（工具使用）这一侧，而不是用在“把代码的下一个 token 预测得更好”这件事上。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Is that roughly the pattern that other founders should have in mind when they're trying to think about where should I use reinforcement learning?
So if you're trying to get an agent to perform tests with tools over long horizon, you need RL.
If you're trying to create a model that's good at summarization or a next token or whatever, you probably don't need RL.
Is is that a good framework for when you need RL?

paragraphs.zh: 这大致是不是其他创业者在思考“我应该把 reinforcement learning 用在哪里”时可以参考的模式？也就是说，如果你是想让一个 agent（智能体）借助工具在长时间跨度上执行测试任务，那你就需要 RL；但如果你只是想做一个擅长 summarization（摘要生成）或者 next token（下一个词元预测）之类的模型，那你可能并不需要 RL。这个框架能不能算是一个判断何时需要 RL 的好方法？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think RL fits everywhere.
So even for tab, we use the RL.
Personally, this is just my theory and it's not backed up by anything.
When you pre train a model, they're just the models are just ingesting the totality of human knowledge.
Let's say you're training a model for math.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得 RL 到处都适用。所以即便是 tab（补全标签/补全功能），我们也会用 RL。就我个人而言，这只是我的一个理论，没有任何严格证据支持。模型在 pre-train（预训练）阶段，本质上只是在吸收整个人类知识的总和。比如说，你在训练一个数学模型。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: The model sort of like learns all the math on stock exchange.

Is it the expert or is it the student that's trying to learn?
And so one of the things that I think happens during RL is that we are tuning this knob, letting the model know, hey, you are the expert.
You need to do things correctly.

paragraphs.zh: 这个模型有点像是把所有数学内容都学进去了。可当它真正面对一道数学题时，如果这是一个还没有经过 RL 的模型，它其实需要先“琢磨”自己到底是哪一种人设：它是 expert（专家），还是一个正在学习的 student（学生）？所以我认为 RL 过程中发生的一件事，就是我们在调这个“旋钮”，让模型知道：嘿，你是专家。你需要把事情做对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So that's like one thing that happens is we are sharpening this distribution.
Sort of like, ERAL has a few phases.
So like there is the very first phase where the model learns and becomes very good very quickly.
And then there is like a second phase where, like, it takes a lot of compute to continuously improve the model and, like, the you see the model starts reasoning and have this pattern.
And so in the very first phase of the curve, I think that's where we're just tuning the knob, telling the model, hey.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，发生的一件事有点像是：我们正在把这个分布“磨尖”。有点像，ERAL 有几个阶段。比如说，第一个阶段是模型学习，并且非常快地变得很强。然后还有第二个阶段，在这个阶段里，要持续改进模型就需要大量 compute（算力），而且你会看到模型开始进行 reasoning（推理），并呈现出这种模式。所以在这条曲线最开始的阶段，我觉得我们其实只是在调那个旋钮，告诉模型，嘿。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You should do things correctly here.
And so RL in the small compute case is also very useful just to let the model know that it has to do things correctly.
That's sort

paragraphs.zh: 你在这里应该把事情做对。所以在小规模 compute 的情况下，RL（强化学习）也非常有用，因为它能让模型知道它必须把事情做正确。大概就是

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: of like my case to this.
Yeah.
I mean, second that, I mean, you we see this pattern across many use cases.

We know we held RL fine tuning generally for many customers, and we see this usually you kind of continuous pre training, basically, retraining, like regular supervised fine tuning is simplifying, you can say, this transfer of new knowledge kind of in abstract way.
And RL is kind of sharpening the behavior or, like, particular qualities you would you would want from from the model.

paragraphs.zh: 我对此的看法。对，我同意这一点。我的意思是，我们在很多 use case（使用场景）里都看到了这种模式。我们知道，我们通常会为很多客户做 RL fine-tuning（强化学习微调），而我们通常看到的是，你某种程度上会做 continuous pre-training（持续预训练），本质上就是 retraining（再训练）；而常规的 supervised fine-tuning（监督微调）可以说是在一种比较抽象的层面上，简化这种新知识的迁移。至于 RL，更像是在打磨模型的行为，或者说你希望模型具备的某些特定品质。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And usually, you end up needing both.

And even to your example of summarization, it's actually like, RL may be very useful for this because sometimes it's if you want particular style out of summarization, right, it's really hard to, like, come up with examples of, like, good and bad summarization, etcetera, like, really describing this precisely.
But if you use, for example, LM as a judge, right, you can actually say very precise rubrics.
You can kind of prompt eval saying like, Okay, this is a criteria how I am going to evaluate whether summarization is good or not.

Throw that into RL loop and let the model kind of experiment with different summarization styles, figure out what you actually want from it, while maybe another LLM kind of evaluates it whether it's mentioned in particular rubric or not.

paragraphs.zh: 而通常，最后你两者都需要。即使拿你说的 summarization（摘要生成）这个例子来说，RL 实际上也可能非常有用，因为有时候如果你想让摘要呈现某种特定风格，对吧，你其实很难举出什么算好的摘要、什么算坏的摘要之类的例子，也很难非常精确地把这个要求描述清楚。但如果你用 LM as a judge（让语言模型充当评审），对吧，你其实可以写出非常精确的 rubrics（评判标准）。你可以通过 prompt eval（提示评估）来说明：好，这是我用来判断摘要好不好的标准。把这些丢进 RL loop（强化学习闭环）里，让模型去尝试不同的摘要风格，摸索出你真正想要它做成什么样；同时也许再让另一个 LLM 来评估它是否满足某条具体 rubric。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And that's kind of type of pattern which you see a lot, not just in coding.

paragraphs.zh: 而这种模式你会经常看到，不只是出现在 coding（编程）里。

paragraphs.zh: 还是说你觉得，LLM as judge，以及其他自动化 rubrics，更有可能把我们带到那个目标？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You don't really, like, put experts directly in judge judging RL rollouts.
I mean, that would be some kind of, like, I mean, real time RL if it's actually users or, like, some form of, like, I really check with DPO.
I mean, generally, the more verifiable your reward is, the better because it allows you to, like, scale the compute and just get better outcome.
In some case and by verifiable, it basically means, like, hey.
How can you automatically produce without the human?

paragraphs.zh: 你其实不会真的把专家直接放进对 RL rollouts 的评判里。我的意思是，那会变成某种……我是说，如果真的是用户参与，那会有点像 real-time RL（实时强化学习），或者某种形式的……这个我得和 DPO 核实一下。总的来说，你的 reward（奖励）越可验证越好，因为这能让你扩展 compute 规模，并获得更好的结果。在某些情况下，所谓“可验证”，基本上就是指：嘿，怎样才能在不依赖人的情况下自动地产生这个东西？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Of course, if it's, like, mess or coding and you can craft something, like, very deterministic, that's the best.

I mean, it's the central for humans.

paragraphs.zh: 当然，如果问题属于那种比较“乱”的场景，或者是 coding，而且你能把它构造成某种非常 deterministic（确定性的）东西，那当然是最好的。LM as a judge（把语言模型当裁判）之所以有效，原因其实有点像 generator-discriminator（生成器-判别器）的区别。也就是说，做判断要容易得多。我是说，这对人类来说也是核心事实，对吧？

paragraphs.zh: 这里没有什么影射。不过，是的，做判断确实容易得多，而且你可以非常精确地设计不同的 criteria（评判标准），用来给某个答案排序。你会看到这样一种模式：你可能会做一个从多个方面构成、非常复杂的 eval（评测）。对吧？因为如果你把多个维度一股脑塞进一个单一元素里，模型可能会搞不清该怎么判断。

sentences.text: Do you think that we're gonna see RL be more effective in the harder to verify domains?
Like, you think LLM is just as sufficient?

paragraphs.zh: 你觉得在那些更难验证的领域里，我们会看到 RL（强化学习）变得更有效吗？还是说，你觉得 LLM（大语言模型）本身就已经足够了？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: That's one of the techniques you would you you would you would start.
Right?
Ideally, you want to figure out what is their outcome, what is their actual metric you want to get.
Right?
So kind of trying to approximate the is one way.

paragraphs.zh: 那会是你开始采用的技术之一，对吧？理想情况下，你还是想弄清楚它们的 outcome（结果）到底是什么，想得到的实际 metric（指标）到底是什么，对吧？所以，某种程度上，尝试去近似它是一种办法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Trying to get bigger simulated environments is another.
Right?
Like, if you can simulate more of your product, if you can simulate more of your environment usually, you have, like, final metric which you care about.
It's just harder to capture.
If you can figure out how to capture this, that's great.

paragraphs.zh: 设法获得更大的 simulated environments（模拟环境）也是另一件事。对吧？比如，如果你能模拟更多你的产品，或者更广泛地模拟你的环境，通常你就会有一个自己真正关心的最终指标，只是这个指标更难被捕捉到。如果你能想办法把它捕捉出来，那就很棒。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And to your point about, you know, experts, I mean, experts are still still needed.
Right?
Because crafting this task and actually encoding the product experience you want, that's that's what matters.
Right?
We went through software one point o, two point o, three point o.

paragraphs.zh: 还有你提到的 experts（专家）这一点，我的意思是，专家仍然还是需要的。对吧？因为设计这个任务，以及真正把你想要的产品体验编码进去，这才是关键。对吧？我们经历过 software one point o、two point o、three point o。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Right?
Because of crafting software directly, we went to crafting training data.
Right now, you're effectively crafting the evaluation rules, but that's still very important.
You need to look at examples.
You need to look at the data.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？因为我们从直接编写软件，转向了构造 training data（训练数据）。而现在，你实际上是在构造 evaluation rules（评估规则），但这依然非常重要。你需要看例子。你需要看数据。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: You need to look at where your product fails and how to nudge the model in the right behavior.

paragraphs.zh: 你需要看你的产品会在哪些地方失败，以及该如何把 model（模型）往正确的行为方向上引导。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I want to ask about RL environments, which is maybe related to what you were talking about.
It seems like there's been a huge explosion in just the revenue scale that some of these RL environments companies are reaching.
What do they provide that's actually useful?
Because I think Cursor, for example, you have so much data on how your customers are actually using your environments.
What do the RL environment vendors offer you on top of what you already have?

paragraphs.zh: 我想问一下 RL environments（强化学习环境），这可能和你刚才说的内容有关。看起来，一些做 RL environments 的公司在收入规模上出现了非常大的爆发式增长。它们到底提供了什么真正有用的东西？因为我觉得，以 Cursor 为例，你们已经有大量关于客户实际如何使用你们环境的数据了。那这些 RL environment vendors（强化学习环境供应商）在你们已有的基础上，还能额外提供什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
We don't actually use any of the environment vendors.
I think so it's very difficult to construct working environments.
It's a valuable product for people that do not, like, have access to this.
However, for coding particularly, there is, a very large amount of working coding environments available to everybody.

paragraphs.zh: 对，我们实际上没有使用任何 environment vendors（环境供应商）。我觉得，构建可用的工作环境其实非常困难。对于那些无法获得这类资源的人来说，这确实是很有价值的产品。不过，特别是在 coding（编程）这个领域，所有人都能获得大量可用的编程环境。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's GitHub.
Right?
You can go in and maybe like you can have a model, like just install all of the dependencies for a repository, and that's like a working environment.
I think a lot of the difficulty comes from the infrastructure as well.
So you can imagine that environment that's that works well for a particular task may need like services up.

paragraphs.zh: 那就是 GitHub。对吧？你可以进去，也许可以让一个 model（模型）把某个 repository（代码仓库）的所有 dependencies（依赖）都装好，那基本上就是一个可用的工作环境了。我认为很多困难也来自 infrastructure（基础设施）。所以你可以想象，一个对特定任务表现良好的环境，可能需要先把一些 services（服务）启动起来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You're like making a change that, let's say, like a database migration to test that is actually working, you need the database up.
Right?
And so those kind of things are very tricky.
I think like these environment companies are like quite helpful for that that kind of stuff.

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，你在做一个改动，假设是一次 database migration（数据库迁移）；要测试它是否真的有效，你就得先把数据库启动起来。对吧？所以这类事情都非常棘手。我觉得这些环境公司在这类问题上还是相当有帮助的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: There are kind of two aspects to this.
Right?
First, like, if you look at like Frontier Labs, right, they're trying to build generic model, which is good at everything.
Right?
So they need to cover all these different tasks underneath, package up in one model, and kind of encourage it to generalize.

paragraphs.zh: 这件事大致有两个方面，对吧？首先，如果你看 Frontier Labs 这类团队，他们是在尝试构建通用模型（generic model），也就是在各种任务上都表现良好的模型。对吧？所以他们需要覆盖底层所有这些不同的任务，把它们打包进一个模型里，并且某种程度上促使它具备泛化能力。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Right?
So that's that's kind of one part, and that's that's very helpful.
Right?
In cases like Composer, right, you have you have your actual product.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？所以这算是其中一部分，而且这非常有帮助。对吧？像 Composer 这样的场景里，你有你实际的产品。对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: And I think that's what it also kind of a bit of Fireworks.
Like, yeah, if you have your actual product, you should you should do well against it.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 而且我觉得这在某种程度上也是 Fireworks 的思路。就是，如果你有你实际的产品，那你就应该围绕它把效果做好。对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: The most powerful environment is your own product.

paragraphs.zh: 最强大的环境就是你自己的产品。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Exactly.
Because, like, that's where your model actually will be used.
And and, of course, if you have your app, you're not gonna do it across all the products.
Right?
But if you're if you're trying to build the best model for your product, specialize and tailor it, You should just use your production environment.

paragraphs.zh: 没错。因为那才是你的模型真正会被使用的地方。当然，如果你有自己的 app，你不会去覆盖所有产品，对吧？但如果你的目标是为你的产品构建最好的模型，对它进行 specialize（专门化）和 tailor（定制），那你就应该直接使用你的生产环境（production environment）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Of course, you want to isolate it properly.
Right?
You don't want to model a wreck havoc on your production database.
You want to clone it, etcetera.
And there are some, you know, tools from environment companies, such as, like, from general infrastructure, which makes it easier.

paragraphs.zh: 当然，你会希望把它正确隔离开。对吧？你不会希望模型把你的生产数据库搞得一团糟。你会想要克隆它，诸如此类。另外，也确实有一些来自环境公司、或者更广义基础设施（infrastructure）领域的工具，能让这件事更容易一些。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: But generally, you want your RL environment to be as close to real production as possible.
Yeah.
And that's what you know, as an example, we see it as if you look at kind of toy RL examples, the toy RL frameworks, they always start with, like, oh, there's this, like, toy environment.
I'm gonna spin up a Docker container and run everything in it, which is great for, like, toy examples if you are trying to teach model how to play Atari or whatever.
Right?

paragraphs.zh: 但总的来说，你会希望你的 RL（强化学习）环境尽可能接近真实生产环境。对。这也是我们看到的一个例子：如果你去看那种玩具级的 RL 示例、玩具级的 RL 框架，它们总是从类似这样的东西开始——哦，这里有一个玩具环境，我要启动一个 Docker container，然后把所有东西都跑在里面。如果你只是想教模型怎么玩 Atari 之类的东西，这对玩具示例来说当然很好。对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: But if you're actually transitioned to, like, production cases, you can't just put your real real production application in a Docker container.
And we found it pretty early ourselves, like, working with many folks.
Like, in case of Courser trainer on their side, some other customers, we run trainer on our training platform.
But for environments, we actually default to running them on the customer side because that's where the actual implementation is.

And you effectively have the same setup of Trainer, even if it's part of Fireworks platform or on the customer side, calling the actual production environment, not trying to kind of wrap it and componentize it

paragraphs.zh: 但如果你真的转向生产级场景，就不能只是把你真实的生产应用塞进一个 Docker container 里。我们自己也很早就发现了这一点，在和很多人合作时就是如此。比如在 Courser trainer 的案例里，或者一些其他客户那里，我们会在自己的训练平台上运行 trainer；但对于 environment（环境），我们的默认做法实际上是在客户那一侧运行，因为真正的实现就在那里。实际上，无论 Trainer 是 Fireworks platform 的一部分，还是部署在客户侧，本质上都是同一种设置：去调用真实的生产环境，而不是试图把它包装（wrap）起来、组件化（componentize）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: On the on the hosted platform because that's really hard and that introduces differences.

paragraphs.zh: 在 hosted platform 上做这件事，是因为那真的很难，而且会引入差异。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Like, I mean, what we call RL environments is really three components.
One is the harness.
So the harness is like where the model can submit tools and the tools get executed.
And the second thing is, let's call it the, like, a kind of operating system.

paragraphs.zh: 对。我的意思是，我们所说的 RL environments（强化学习环境）其实由三个部分组成。第一部分是 harness，也就是模型可以提交 tools（工具），然后这些工具会被执行的地方。第二部分，我们可以把它叫作一种 operating system（操作系统）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right?
So like, what is the actual, like, world, the the in state where the model is like interacting with.
And then there is like the reward component.
We need which needs to check at the end that the work is is done correctly.
And generally, the harness is pretty portable.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？也就是说，模型实际在与之交互的那个世界、那个内部状态到底是什么。然后还有 reward component（奖励组件）。我们需要它在最后检查工作是否被正确完成。而一般来说，harness 是相当可移植的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You can take the harness and put in in many different environments.
The thing that's key is the operating system.
And to replicate this, just normal containers don't really work very well.
So at Cursor, we actually built, a whole virtual machine stack, and so we can spin up, like, virtual machines really quickly.
And it has to be super bursty because you can imagine, like, we are asking this system, please give me a 100,000 virtual machines now, And it has to come all come up and

paragraphs.zh: 你可以把 harness 拿出来，放进很多不同的 environments（环境）里。关键在于 operating system。要复现这一点，普通的 containers（容器）其实并不是很好用。所以在 Cursor，我们实际上构建了整套 virtual machine（虚拟机）技术栈，这样我们就能非常快速地启动 virtual machines。而且它必须具备很强的 bursty（突发式扩缩）能力，因为你可以想象，我们是在对这个系统说：请现在给我 100,000 台 virtual machines，而且它们都必须全部启动起来，并且

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: yeah.
Awesome.
I really enjoyed this conversation today.
I think Cursor is such an inspiration in what you all are doing as a company towards going from application company to really a frontier model lab.
And think I the work you did with Composer two really leads that charge.

paragraphs.zh: 对，太棒了。我今天真的很享受这次对话。我觉得 Cursor 作为一家公司，你们正在做的事情非常鼓舞人心——从一家 application company（应用公司）走向真正的 frontier model lab（前沿模型实验室）。而且我认为，你们在 Composer two 上所做的工作，确实在引领这一进程。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So really special to hear about it.
And then, Dima, really cool to hear about the hardcore infrastructure problems actually that the two of you solved together in the trenches over many, many late nights to make it all possible.
So thank you thank you guys for joining today.

paragraphs.zh: 所以能听到这些真的很特别。还有，Dima，也很高兴听到那些非常硬核的基础设施问题——你们两位一起在一线、熬过许多个深夜，才把这一切真正做成。所以感谢你们今天来参加。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Thank you so much for having us.
Take care.

paragraphs.zh: 非常感谢邀请我们。保重。
